metadata.title: Propensity Score Weighting: Balancing the Groups
metadata.topic: CAUSAL MACHINE LEARNING — PROPENSITY SCORE WEIGHTING

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Olá, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.topic: CAUSAL MACHINE LEARNING — PROPENSITY SCORE WEIGHTING
props.segment: Propensity Score Weighting: Balancing the Groups

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Treated users are younger and wealthier. Controls are older and poorer. Naive comparison is useless. Propensity weighting re-balances the groups.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'propensity weighting — make the groups look alike'; treatment imbalance vs causal estimate

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Generate observational data with severe treatment/control imbalance, estimate propensity scores, compute IPW-weighted ATE, and print SMD before and after weighting.

props.command: claude "Write a Python IPW demo: observational n=3000, age~N(40,10), income~N(50k,15k). P(T=1|X) = sigmoid(-2 + 0.05*age + 0.00003*income). True ATE=3.0. Y = 3*T + 0.1*age + 0.00002*income + noise. Fit logistic regression for propensity. Compute: naive ATE, IPW ATE. Print SMD for age and income before/after weighting."

beats.narration_text: Read it. IPW works by down-weighting controls who look nothing like the treated and up-weighting those who do. The propensity score is the balancing score — conditioning on it is equivalent to conditioning on all confounders.

props.code:
# ipw_demo.py — Claude Code output

# Estimate propensity scores
prop_model = LogisticRegression().fit(X, T)
ps = prop_model.predict_proba(X)[:,1]

# IPW weights: 1/p(T|X) for treated, 1/(1-p(T|X)) for control
w = np.where(T==1, 1/ps, 1/(1-ps))

# IPW-weighted ATE
ipw_ate = (np.sum(w * T * Y) / np.sum(w * T)) \
        - (np.sum(w * (1-T) * Y) / np.sum(w * (1-T)))

# SMD = |mu_T - mu_C| / sqrt((sd_T^2 + sd_C^2) / 2)

beats.narration_text: Left panel: SMD balance plot. Age SMD drops from 0.45 to 0.06. Income SMD drops from 0.52 to 0.08 — both below the 0.1 threshold. Right panel: naive ATE at 3.72, IPW ATE at 3.05, truth at 3.00. IPW bias is 0.05. Naive bias is 0.72.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: SMD balance bars left + ATE number line right

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to add overlap trimming — drop units with extreme propensity scores below 0.05 or above 0.95. Extreme weights amplify noise. Trimming trades a little coverage for a lot of variance reduction.

props.command:
claude "update ipw_demo.py:
  -> add overlap trimming: drop units with ps < 0.05 or ps > 0.95
  -> compare trimmed IPW ATE vs untrimmed
  -> show how trimming affects effective sample size and SE"

beats.narration_text: Trimming drops 8 percent of units but cuts the IPW standard error by 35 percent. Trimmed ATE is 3.03 — closer to truth and more stable across seeds. The overlap condition is not a technicality — it determines whether causal estimation is possible.
beats.visual_intent: slate: trimmed vs untrimmed IPW ATE comparison; propensity score histogram with trimming thresholds

beats.narration_text: IPW is a reweighting trick that makes your observational data look like an experiment. The propensity score collapses all confounders into one number. The SMD below 0.1 on all covariates is your evidence that it worked.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: IPW formula, SMD threshold 0.1, overlap condition

beats.narration_text: Your move: check the SMD table on your observational dataset before and after any propensity adjustment. If any covariate has SMD above 0.2 after weighting, your propensity model is misspecified. The next reel covers doubly robust estimation — AIPW — which protects against that misspecification.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: run SMD check, identify misspecified propensity, proceed to AIPW reel

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. IPW is a reweighting trick that makes your observational data look like an experiment. The propensity score collapses all confounders into one number. The SMD below 0.1 on all covariates is your evidence that it worked. Your move: check the SMD table on your observational dataset before and after any propensity adjustment. If any covariate has SMD above 0.2 after weighting, your propensity model is misspecified. The next reel covers doubly robust estimation — AIPW — which protects against that misspecification.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Propensity Score Weighting: Balancing the Groups] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
